Spokane's fight for reasonable
freight rates against the unjust dis
ordinations of the Northern Pa
cific, Great Northern, Union Pacific
and o. r. & N. system, in favor OF
coast points, began at 10 o'clock
this morning in Judge Remnants de-
partment of the superior court.


Commissioners Prouty and Lane,
of the interstate commerce commits
sion, presided.


Just before the noon hour the city
introduced F. H. McEwen, former
chief clerk and commercial agent
of the Great Northern railway, who
had the effect Of a bomb thrown
into the camp Of the railroads
There followed a scurrying and
whispering among the attorneys OF
the latter, who evidently did not
count upon the city producing a
witness who has been so closely In
touch with the inner workings OF
the railroads. Until the morning
session closed at 1890 O'Clock Mr..
McEwen put in the time designating
and explaining a voluminous set OF
rates now in effect and explaining
the differentials whereby Spokane
was Disseminated against.


The hearing began with Commits.
sioner Prouty's explanation that all
proceedings were to be extremely
informal, as the commission only
wanted to get at the facts as ex
peditiously as possible.


Brooks Adam, of Boston, made
the opening statement for the city
in which he styled the railroads as
the most domineering Of all high
ways, with the power Of exactly
from the public any money II
pleased, unless they were interfered
with by competition or the inter
vention of the government. He
styled the railroads as public tax
collectors and said they stand in
the same position as a tax collector
appropriating the public's money tc
their own enterprises and thereby
becoming embeZzlers. They are
justified in reserving a reasonable
amount Of money on their invest
ment as fund against deprecia
ton, but anything beyond that they
are accountable for to the public
in the relation of guardians. He re
hearsed the history of railroad
building in the West and told how
he admired James j. Hill and the
way he had established himself and
his interests. He said Hill hat
agreed to give Spokane as good
rates as any city in the West, and
received in return great rights OF
way and terminal sites so as tc
make Hill impregnable. Hill did not
carry out this contract. He found
it more profitable to keep high
rate and pocket the money.


Adams told Of the conspiracy
whereby Hill got control of the
Northern Pacific and the Burlington
and thereby became the absolute
monarch OF this section, skillfully
fencing in Spokane. Hill took out
s17,300.000 Of which he is account
able for to the people.


Spokane was absolutely in Hills
power and there was no means OF
resistance until the interstate com.
merce laws passed. Hill has exer.
cised an absolute despotism here
Adams begged the commissioners
to do justice between Spokane and
those in power. He said the pres
